required.title: [Reference] A comprehensive dictionary with etymological information

suggested.title: [Read] English Vocabulary Elements
suggested.author: Keith Denning, Brett Kessler, and William R. Leben

supplementary.title: [Reference] NTC's Dictionary of Latin and Greek Origins
supplementary.author: Bob Moore and Maxine Moore

competence_markers.description: The student can identify and explain at least 30 Latin and Greek roots and generate multiple derivative words for each
The student can break unfamiliar multimorphemic words into parts and produce accurate meaning estimates
The student systematically combines root knowledge with context clues when encountering unfamiliar vocabulary in reading
The student can produce the complete Context-Based 150 in their primary target language with pronunciation that is understood by native speakers
The student can complete the "survive a day" simulation in their target language
The student has begun building survival vocabulary (first 40-60 items) in a second operational language
The student maintains a daily spaced repetition practice for vocabulary in both English and the target language
The student is building domain-specific vocabulary from other framework packets and connecting it to etymological knowledge

narration_prompts.prompt: Take this word apart and tell me its story:
narration_prompts.detail: [give a multimorphemic word like "unprecedented," "intercontinental," or "photosynthesis"]. What are the pieces? What do they mean? What does the whole word mean? Where does it come from?

narration_prompts.prompt: You encounter a word you have never seen in a science text. Walk me through your process for figuring out what it means.
narration_prompts.detail: What do you look at first? What strategies do you use?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why does English have so many words for the same thing?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me examples of Anglo-Saxon, Latin, and Greek words that overlap in meaning. Why does the "level" of the word matter?
Pick three categories and give me the key items in your target language. Why are these categories organized this way?

narration_prompts.prompt: Complete the "survive a morning" scenario in your target language.
narration_prompts.detail: [Mentor plays the role of hotel staff, waiter, bus driver, etc.]

narration_prompts.prompt: What is spaced repetition and why does it work?
narration_prompts.detail: How are you using it? What would happen to your vocabulary if you stopped?

cross_connections.description: Morphological analysis in 3.1.1 (affixation, derivation, compounding) is the formal linguistic framework for the etymology studied here. The student who understands that "-tion" is a nominalizing suffix (it turns verbs into nouns) has linguistic precision that deepens their etymological analysis.

When learning the Context-Based 150 in languages with non-Latin scripts, script literacy from 3.1.2 is directly required. The student who can read Cyrillic, Arabic, or Devanagari can study their target language's survival vocabulary in its native script — not just in transliteration.

Cognate networks across related languages — the sound correspondences that reveal genetic relationships — are vocabulary accelerants. The student who knows that Latin "p" often corresponds to English "f" (pater/father, piscis/fish) can predict English cognates from Latin roots.

Historical vocabulary (dynasty, republic, feudalism, colonialism) is Latin and Greek in origin. The student building domain vocabulary from history packets is simultaneously building etymological knowledge.

Scientific terminology is overwhelmingly Greek. The student who knows "bio" (life), "geo" (earth), "phot" (light), "therm" (heat), "hydr" (water), and "morph" (form) can decode most scientific terms they will encounter through Weathering Storms.

The Context-Based 150 is directly operational in 2.4.6's urban survival scenarios. The student who can say "help," "hospital," "I am allergic to," and "where is the embassy" in a foreign language during an urban survival exercise is integrating 3.1.3 with 2.4.

Daily spaced repetition practice is a discipline challenge. The system works only if used consistently. This is where vocabulary building meets character formation — the student who maintains their practice when it is boring and inconvenient is building discipline that transfers everywhere.

content.why_this_matters:
You have spent Stretching Branches waking up to words — noticing them, collecting them, beginning to see that they are built from parts and that knowing those parts gives you power over unfamiliar language. Now you are going to systematize that knowledge. You are going to move from occasional word analysis to a disciplined practice that will expand your vocabulary faster than any other method available to you.

This packet has two major components. The first is **deep etymology** — not just recognizing that "aqua" means water, but understanding how Latin and Greek roots combine, mutate, and layer to produce the vast majority of English academic, scientific, legal, and professional vocabulary. You will learn enough roots, prefixes, and suffixes to decode most unfamiliar words you encounter in serious reading — not perfectly every time, but well enough to make informed guesses that context clues can confirm.

The second component is the **Context-Based 150** — the survival and functional vocabulary system that gives you the minimum effective word set to operate in any language environment. In Stretching Branches, you learned a handful of words and phrases. Now you are going to learn the complete system: 150 carefully chosen items organized by function that cover the vast majority of survival situations. You will master these in your primary target language and begin building them in a second language.

The principle behind both components is the same: **strategic vocabulary acquisition.** Not learning every word — that is impossible and unnecessary. Learning the right words, in the right order, for maximum real-world impact. The person who knows 150 precisely chosen words in a foreign language is more functional than the person who knows 500 randomly chosen ones. The person who knows 30 Latin and Greek roots can decode thousands of English words. Strategy beats volume every time.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Systematic Latin and Greek root knowledge** — at least 50 roots with their derivative word families
2. **Advanced prefix and suffix mastery** — including negative prefixes, intensifiers, and derivational suffixes that change word class
3. **Context clue sophistication** — combining root knowledge with contextual reasoning for reliable word decoding
4. **Domain-specific vocabulary** — building word sets for science, history, law, and other fields encountered across the framework
5. **The complete Context-Based 150** — survival, navigation, transaction, social, emergency, relational, and connector vocabulary in the primary target language
6. **Spaced repetition as a vocabulary system** — the science of long-term retention applied to language learning
7. **Beginning second-language survival vocabulary** — the first 40-60 items in a second operational language
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Architecture of English Vocabulary

English vocabulary has layers, like geological strata. Each layer was deposited by a different historical event:

**The Anglo-Saxon layer** (Old English, approximately 450-1100 CE): These are the short, common, everyday words — the ones you learned first as a child. House, eat, drink, sleep, hand, foot, sun, moon, love, hate, good, bad, live, die. Roughly 25% of English words are Anglo-Saxon in origin, but they account for about 70% of the words in everyday speech. They are the foundation.

**The French/Latin layer** (approximately 1066 onwards, after the Norman Conquest): When French-speaking Normans conquered England, French — which descended from Latin — flooded into English. This layer gave English most of its "formal" vocabulary. Where Anglo-Saxon has "buy," French/Latin gives "purchase." Where Anglo-Saxon has "ask," French/Latin gives "inquire." Where Anglo-Saxon has "start," French/Latin gives "commence." This is why English often has two words for the same thing — one casual, one formal. The casual word is usually Anglo-Saxon. The formal word is usually French or Latin.

**The Greek layer** (primarily from the Renaissance onwards): When European scholars rediscovered Greek texts and began building modern science, they built their technical vocabulary from Greek roots. Biology, psychology, philosophy, democracy, technology, microscope, telephone, photography — all Greek. This layer is densest in academic, scientific, and medical vocabulary.

**The borrowing layer** (ongoing): English borrows freely from every language it contacts. "Tsunami" from Japanese. "Safari" from Swahili. "Algorithm" from Arabic (from the name of the mathematician al-Khwarizmi). "Chocolate" from Nahuatl. "Yoga" from Sanskrit. "Kindergarten" from German. These borrowings are a living record of English speakers' contact with other cultures.

Understanding these layers tells you something important: **the register of a word — how formal or informal it feels — is largely determined by which layer it comes from.** Anglo-Saxon words feel direct and plain. Latin/French words feel elevated and formal. Greek words feel technical and scholarly. A skilled writer or speaker moves between layers deliberately, choosing the register that fits the context.

### Part 2: Fifty Roots That Unlock the Language

In Stretching Branches, you learned ten roots. Now you are going to expand to fifty — a collection that gives you analytical access to the majority of academic English vocabulary. These are organized by origin and meaning cluster.

**Latin Roots — Actions and Processes:**

| Root | Meaning | Examples |
|------|---------|----------|
| **duc/duct** | lead | conduct, educate, introduce, produce, reduce |
| **fac/fect** | make, do | factory, effect, perfect, manufacture, defect |
| **mitt/miss** | send | transmit, mission, dismiss, missile, admission |
| **pon/pos** | place, put | position, compose, deposit, opponent, impose |
| **tract** | pull, drag | attract, contract, extract, subtract, tractor |
| **vert/vers** | turn | convert, reverse, universe, controversy, divert |
| **cred** | believe | credit, incredible, credible, credentials |
| **junct** | join | junction, conjunction, adjunct, injunction |
| **reg** | rule, guide | regulate, region, regime, regular |
| **cap/cept** | take, seize | capture, accept, concept, intercept, capable |

**Latin Roots — States and Qualities:**

| Root | Meaning | Examples |
|------|---------|----------|
| **form** | shape | inform, reform, transform, uniform, formula |
| **fort** | strong | fortress, fortify, effort, comfortable, force |
| **gen** | birth, kind | generate, gentle, gender, gene, generous |
| **mag/max** | great | magnitude, maximum, magnify, magnificent |
| **min** | small, less | minimum, diminish, minor, minute, minister |
| **nov** | new | novel, innovate, renovate, novice |
| **temp** | time | temporary, contemporary, tempo, temporal |
| **vit/viv** | life | vital, vivid, survive, vitamin, revive |

**Latin Roots — Perception and Knowledge:**

| Root | Meaning | Examples |
|------|---------|----------|
| **cogn** | know | recognize, cognition, incognito, cognitive |
| **loc** | place | locate, local, allocate, locomotive, dislocate |
| **mem** | remember | memory, memorial, commemorate, memoir |
| **nomin/nom** | name | nominate, nominal, denomination, noun |
| **sens/sent** | feel | sense, sentiment, consent, sensitive, sentence |
| **sign** | mark | signature, signal, significant, design, assign |
| **voc/vok** | voice, call | vocabulary, invoke, advocate, vocal, provoke |

**Greek Roots — Science and Knowledge:**

| Root | Meaning | Examples |
|------|---------|----------|
| **bio** | life | biology, biography, antibiotic, symbiosis |
| **chron** | time | chronicle, chronological, synchronize, chronic |
| **geo** | earth | geography, geology, geometry, geopolitical |
| **hydr** | water | hydrate, dehydrate, hydroelectric, hydrogen |
| **log/logy** | word, study | logic, biology, dialogue, monologue, technology |
| **morph** | form, shape | morphology, metamorphosis, amorphous |
| **path** | feeling, suffering | sympathy, empathy, pathology, apathy |
| **phil** | love | philosophy, philanthropy, Philadelphia |
| **phon** | sound | telephone, phonetic, symphony, microphone |
| **phot/phos** | light | photograph, phosphorus, photosynthesis |
| **psych** | mind, soul | psychology, psychic, psychiatry |
| **scope** | look, see | microscope, telescope, scope, horoscope |
| **therm** | heat | thermometer, thermal, thermostat, hypothermia |

**Key Prefixes:**

| Prefix | Meaning | Examples |
|--------|---------|----------|
| **anti-** | against | antibiotic, antisocial, antidote |
| **auto-** | self | automatic, automobile, autobiography |
| **co-/com-/con-** | together, with | cooperate, combine, connect |
| **de-** | down, away, undo | decrease, depart, deactivate |
| **dis-** | not, apart | disagree, disappear, disconnect |
| **ex-** | out, former | exit, export, exclude, ex-president |
| **inter-** | between | international, interrupt, interact |
| **micro-** | small | microscope, microphone, microchip |
| **mono-** | one | monologue, monarchy, monopoly |
| **poly-** | many | polygon, polyglot, polysyllabic |
| **sub-** | under | submarine, subtract, subterranean |
| **super-** | above, beyond | superhero, supernatural, supervise |
| **trans-** | across | transport, translate, transfer |

You do not memorize this chart in one sitting. You work through it over weeks, encountering each root in real reading, building word families, and adding items to your spaced repetition deck. The goal is recognition in context, not recitation from memory.

### Part 3: Context Clues as a System

In Stretching Branches, you learned to look for context clues when you encountered unfamiliar words. Now you are going to understand the different types of context clues and use them systematically alongside your root knowledge.

**Definition clues** — the text directly defines the word: "The archaeologist found an artifact — an object made by humans long ago."

**Example clues** — the text gives examples that reveal meaning: "The fauna of the island included parrots, lizards, and giant tortoises." (Fauna = animal life)

**Contrast clues** — the text contrasts the word with something known: "Unlike her gregarious sister who loved parties, Maria was reserved and preferred to be alone." (Gregarious = sociable)

**Inference clues** — the meaning is implied by the overall context: "After three days without food, the survivors were ravenous." (The context of starvation implies extreme hunger)

**Root + Context combined** — the most powerful approach: you recognize a root that gives you a partial meaning, and the context narrows it to the specific meaning. You encounter "subterranean": you know "sub" (under) and "terra" (earth), so it means "under the earth." The context — "subterranean tunnels" — confirms: underground tunnels.

The skilled reader combines all these strategies automatically. That automaticity is what this packet builds through practice.

### Part 4: Domain-Specific Vocabulary

Different fields of knowledge use different specialized vocabularies. As you work through the Evergreen Homeschool framework, you encounter vocabulary from many domains:

**History:** civilization, empire, dynasty, monarchy, revolution, colonialism, sovereignty, feudalism
**Science:** hypothesis, experiment, organism, photosynthesis, ecosystem, element, compound
**Law and government:** constitution, legislation, jurisdiction, amendment, sovereignty, democracy
**Philosophy:** ethics, logic, metaphysics, epistemology, dialectic, empirical
**Mathematics:** equation, variable, function, theorem, algorithm, probability

Notice how many of these are Latin and Greek in origin. The roots you are learning in this packet are the keys to all of these domains. The student who knows "sover" comes from the Latin "super" (above) and connects it to "sovereign" — one who is above, who rules themselves — has understood a word that is central to this entire framework.

You should be building domain-specific vocabulary naturally as you work through other packets. When you encounter a new technical term in 1.1 (history), 1.6 (science), 1.4 (government), or any other sub-domain, add it to your vocabulary system — look up its etymology, connect it to roots you know, and place it in your spaced repetition deck.

### Part 5: The Context-Based 150

The Context-Based 150 is a vocabulary system designed for functional survival in any language. It is organized not by grammar category (nouns, verbs, adjectives) but by **what you need to do.** Here is the complete system:

**Survival Core (20 items):** water, food, sleep, hot, cold, sick, pain, help, stop, go, yes, no, good, bad, big, small, here, there, now, later

**Navigation (20 items):** where, left, right, straight, near, far, map, street, bus, train, airport, hotel, north, south, east, west, how far, how long, lost, return

**Transaction (20 items):** how much, too expensive, cheaper, buy, sell, pay, money, one through ten, hundred, thousand, receipt, change, credit card, market

**Social Core (25 items):** hello, goodbye, please, thank you, excuse me, sorry, my name is, your name, where from, how are you, fine/well, beautiful, delicious, friend, family, welcome, congratulations, cheers, I like, I don't like, happy, sad, tired, interesting, funny

**Emergency (15 items):** help, police, hospital, doctor, fire, accident, thief, embassy, phone, emergency, allergic, medicine, danger, lost, injured

**Relational (25 items):** I understand, I don't understand, please speak slowly, can you repeat, how do you say, what does this mean, I am learning your language, I speak a little, is this correct, teach me, I'm trying, do you speak English, I need, I want, can I, may I, where is, when, why, how, who, what, which, this one, that one

**Connectors (25 items):** and, or, but, because, if, then, also, very, not, more, less, before, after, with, without, maybe, always, never, sometimes, already, still, only, all, some, none

These 150 items, learned with correct pronunciation and used with appropriate cultural framing, cover the vast majority of survival and basic social situations. A person with these 150 words can eat, sleep, travel, buy things, ask for help, handle emergencies, and begin building relationships.

You are going to master the complete 150 in your primary target language. This means not just recognizing the words but producing them under real-time communicative pressure — in a role-play, in a simulated scenario, or ideally in a real interaction with native speakers.

### Part 6: Spaced Repetition — The Science of Not Forgetting

Vocabulary you learn and never review fades. Within 24 hours of learning a new word, you forget roughly 70% of it unless you review. Within a week, it is nearly gone. This is the forgetting curve, and it applies to everyone.

Spaced repetition is the antidote. The principle is simple: review a new item shortly after learning it, then at increasing intervals — one day, then three days, then a week, then two weeks, then a month. Each successful review pushes the next review further into the future. Each failure shortens the interval. Over time, well-reviewed items move into long-term memory and need only occasional maintenance.

Tools like Anki automate this process. You create flashcards (word on one side, definition/translation on the other), and the software schedules your reviews based on your performance. Daily practice of 15-20 minutes maintains and steadily grows your vocabulary in both English and your target language.

This is not optional. It is the difference between vocabulary that sticks and vocabulary that evaporates. The student who uses spaced repetition consistently for six months will retain more vocabulary than the student who studies intensively for a month and then stops.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Root Family Expansion [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Work through the root chart systematically, approximately 3-5 roots per week. For each root, the student builds a word family of at least five words, looks up the etymology of each, and adds the family to their spaced repetition deck. The goal is not to finish the chart quickly but to build genuine recognition that transfers to real reading.

### Study 2: The Word Archaeology Project [Early Packet]
Choose ten words from a challenging text the student is currently reading (history, science, literature). For each word, trace its etymology back to its root language. Map the journey: "democracy" from Greek "demos" (people) + "kratos" (power/rule) — literally "rule by the people." This exercise makes etymology real by connecting it to actual reading, not abstract exercises.

### Study 3: Context-Based 150 — Systematic Acquisition [Ongoing]
Work through the 150 by category, starting with Survival Core and Social Core (the highest-priority items). Learn 10-15 items per week in the target language, with pronunciation drilled from native-speaker audio. Add each item to the spaced repetition deck. Test progress through the simulated scenarios below.

### Study 4: The "Survive a Morning" Simulation [Mid-Packet]
Role-play a half-day scenario in the target language: wake up in a hotel in a country where the target language is spoken. Ask about breakfast. Order food. Ask where the bus stop is. Buy a bus ticket. Ask for directions to a museum. Handle a minor problem (you cannot find your hotel key; you feel sick). This simulation tests whether the vocabulary is functional — not just recognized but produced under communicative pressure.

### Study 5: Domain Vocabulary Audit [Mid-to-Late Packet]
The student reviews vocabulary encountered in other framework packets (history, science, government, etc.) and identifies words with Latin or Greek roots. They trace the etymology and connect the words to roots they have learned. This audit reveals how much of academic vocabulary is accessible through the root system.

### Study 6: Second Language Survival Core [Late Packet]
Begin building the first 40-60 items of the Context-Based 150 in a second operational language. Start with Survival Core and Social Core. The process is faster the second time because the organizational framework is already internalized — the student knows which categories matter and in what order.

### Study 7: The "Survive a Day" Full Simulation [End of Packet]
The full simulation: arrive at an airport, navigate transportation, check into a hotel, order food, ask directions, shop at a market, handle a minor medical issue, and engage in basic social conversation — all in the target language. This is the capstone assessment of functional vocabulary in the primary operational language.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Etymological Analysis
**Developing well:** The student spontaneously breaks unfamiliar words into morphological parts. They connect new roots to known word families. When reading challenging text, they reach for etymological analysis as a first strategy. Their analyses are accurate more often than not.

**Developing slowly:** The student can recite roots from flashcards but does not apply the knowledge to real reading. Unfamiliar words in context are still treated as obstacles to skip rather than puzzles to solve.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you providing enough practice with the combined strategy (roots + context) on real texts? Are the texts the student reads challenging enough to contain unfamiliar words regularly? Has the student moved beyond root memorization to root application?

### 2. Context-Based 150 — Functional Production
**Developing well:** The student can produce the 150 items in their target language with pronunciation understood by native speakers. They can use these items in simulated scenarios fluidly — not perfectly, but communicatively. They recover when they forget a word by rephrasing or using gestures.

**Developing slowly:** The student can recognize the 150 items in written form or on flashcards but cannot produce them in real-time conversation. Pronunciation is not intelligible. Simulations break down quickly.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student practicing production (speaking) or only reception (recognizing)? These are different skills and must be trained separately. Is pronunciation being trained from native-speaker audio or only from text? Is simulation practice happening regularly, or only as a test at the end?

### 3. Spaced Repetition Discipline
**Developing well:** The student reviews daily. Deck statistics show consistent practice and steady accuracy improvement. New cards are being added regularly from both English vocabulary and target language study.

**Developing slowly:** Reviews are sporadic. Large backlogs accumulate. The student treats spaced repetition as a chore to be avoided rather than a tool to be used.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the daily review session at a fixed, sustainable time? Is the deck too large, creating review sessions that feel overwhelming? (If so, reduce new card intake until the backlog is managed.) Does the student understand why spaced repetition works — have you explained the forgetting curve and the science behind it? Understanding the reason often improves compliance.

### 4. Domain Vocabulary Integration
**Developing well:** The student connects vocabulary from other framework domains to their etymological knowledge. They note Latin and Greek roots in history, science, and philosophy texts. Their domain vocabulary journal is growing across multiple fields.

**Developing slowly:** Vocabulary knowledge stays compartmentalized. The student does not connect etymology to other reading. Domain-specific words are treated as isolated items rather than members of root families.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you explicitly making these connections when they arise in other domain work? "You just read 'democracy' in your government packet — what roots do you see?" The connections are there, but the student may need prompting to notice them until the habit becomes automatic.

### 5. Second Language Survival Core
**Developing well:** The student has the first 40-60 items of the Context-Based 150 in a second language. Pronunciation is acceptable. They can handle basic survival scenarios (greetings, ordering food, asking for help) in this second language.

**Developing slowly:** The second language has not started, or only the Big Five are in place. No functional phrases beyond greetings.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is there a daily practice slot for the second language, even a brief one? Has a good audio resource been identified? Is the student motivated by the choice of language, or is it imposed?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Treating the 150 as a Vocabulary List
The Context-Based 150 is not a list to memorize — it is a functional toolkit to deploy. If the student can recite "how much, too expensive, cheaper" from a flashcard but cannot use these phrases in a market simulation, the training has failed. Always train for production in context, not recognition in isolation.

### Root Memorization Without Application
Fifty roots on flashcards, perfectly memorized, are worthless if the student does not use them to decode real words in real reading. Application is the entire point. If the student treats roots as a separate subject from their actual reading, something structural needs to change — probably more explicit practice of the combined strategy on authentic texts.

### Letting Spaced Repetition Lapse
A one-week lapse in spaced repetition creates a review backlog that feels punishing. A one-month lapse effectively resets the entire system. If the student struggles with daily compliance, adjust the system rather than abandoning it: reduce new card intake, set a shorter daily review time, or find a different time slot. The system must be sustainable or it fails.

### Neglecting Pronunciation
A student who knows 150 words in their target language but pronounces them in a way native speakers cannot understand has not acquired functional vocabulary. Pronunciation training must be part of the acquisition process, not an afterthought. Native-speaker audio is essential. If the student is training from text alone, their pronunciation will calcify around English sound patterns and become progressively harder to correct.

### Over-Focusing on One Domain
The student may gravitate toward the vocabulary of their favorite domain — science words if they love science, history words if they love history. Encourage breadth. The student who knows scientific Greek but not legal Latin has a gap. Domain vocabulary should grow across all fields the student encounters in the framework.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Word Archaeology
**Strong:** The student breaks the word accurately into morphological parts, identifies the root and any affixes, traces the etymology to the source language, and synthesizes a clear meaning that matches the actual definition. They show awareness of how the word's current meaning connects to its original roots.
**Weak:** The student guesses at the meaning without systematic analysis. They cannot identify the root or identify it incorrectly. The etymology is vague or invented.

### Prompt 2: Process Description
**Strong:** The student articulates a clear multi-step process: check for recognizable roots, identify prefix and suffix, form a hypothesis, check against context, look it up if still uncertain. They describe this as a system, not a one-off strategy.
**Weak:** "I guess." Or describes only one strategy (context only, dictionary only) without integration.

### Prompt 3: Vocabulary Layers
**Strong:** The student explains the Anglo-Saxon/Latin/Greek layers with examples, connecting formality level to etymological origin. They can give pairs (buy/purchase, ask/inquire, start/commence) and explain when you would use each.
**Weak:** Cannot explain why English has duplicate vocabulary. Does not connect word origin to register.

### Prompt 4: Context-Based 150
**Strong:** The student explains the organizational principle (function over grammar category) and demonstrates items from multiple categories with correct pronunciation. They can explain why this organization is more useful for survival than traditional vocabulary lists.
**Weak:** Can recite items from one category only. Cannot explain the organizational principle. Pronunciation is unintelligible.

### Prompt 5: Simulation Performance
**Strong:** The student navigates the scenario with functional success — they accomplish the tasks (ordering food, asking directions, handling a problem) even if their language is imperfect. They rephrase when stuck. They use gestures and context. They persist through confusion.
**Weak:** The student freezes when they cannot produce a word. They give up or switch to English immediately. They cannot accomplish basic tasks despite having studied the vocabulary.

### Prompt 6: Spaced Repetition
**Strong:** The student explains the forgetting curve, the principle of increasing intervals, and describes their personal practice. They can cite their retention statistics and growth rate.
**Weak:** "I do my flashcards." No understanding of why the system works or how to optimize it.
